name: Up to Serra das Flores — circular route
name_original: Até a Serra das Flores — percurso circular
region_intro: No traffic touring cycling routes around the Duero traverse a landscape defined by the Duero River, which carves dramatic canyons and deep gorges, particularly in areas like the Arribes del Duero Natural Park. The region transitions from terraced vineyards in the west to more arid plains with olive and almond groves further east. Cyclists will encounter varied terrain, including serene riverside paths, rolling hills, and sections through natural reserves. Best no traffic touring cycling routes around the Duero The most popular no traffic touring cycling route is River Pisuerga bank – circular from Tordesillas via Robladillo (EV1), a 27.0 miles (43.5 km) trail that takes 3 hours 12 minutes to complete. This moderate route follows the tranquil banks of the River Pisuerga. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Valle del Cuco – circular desde Curiel de Duero, a moderate 27.3 miles (43.9 km) path. This route explores the scenic Valle del Cuco, offering views of the surrounding countryside. Local touring cyclists also love the Rio Duero from Toro - circular via San Jose canal - Reserva natural de las Riberas de Castronuño-Vega del Duero, a 25.6 miles (41.2 km) trail leading through the Reserva natural de las Riberas de Castronuño-Vega del Duero, often completed in about 2 hours 59 minutes. Touring cycling around the Duero is defined by river canyons, terraced vineyards, and arid plains. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy riverside paths to more challenging routes through hilly terrain. The routes in Duero are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from more than 300 reviews. More than 8000 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Duero's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many no-traffic touring cycling routes are available in the Duero region?
A: There are over 200 dedicated no-traffic touring cycling routes in the Duero region, offering a wide variety of experiences. These routes are designed to provide peaceful rides away from vehicular traffic, allowing you to fully immerse yourself in the stunning landscapes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on these no-traffic cycling routes in the Duero region?
A: The Duero region offers a diverse range of terrain, even on its no-traffic routes. You'll find everything from relatively flat riverside paths along the Duero and Pisuerga rivers to more challenging routes with moderate elevation gains through rolling hills and vineyard landscapes. Some sections might feature unpaved surfaces, typical of rural tracks, while others are smoothly paved.

Q: Are there any circular no-traffic touring routes in the Duero region?
A: Yes, many of the no-traffic touring cycling routes in the Duero region are circular, making them convenient for day trips. For example, you can enjoy the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1175321922/river-pisuerga-bank-circular-from-tordesillas-via-robladillo-ev1" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;River Pisuerga bank – circular from Tordesillas via Robladillo&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1175293169/valle-del-cuco-circular-desde-curiel-de-duero" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valle del Cuco – circular desde Curiel de Duero&lt;/a&gt;, both offering beautiful loops through the countryside.

Q: What are the difficulty levels of the no-traffic touring cycling routes?
A: The no-traffic touring cycling routes in the Duero region cater to various skill levels. You'll find 28 easy routes, perfect for a relaxed ride, 108 moderate routes for those seeking a bit more challenge, and 74 difficult routes for experienced cyclists looking for significant elevation changes and longer distances. An example of an easy route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1036861878/fuentecantos-and-chavaler-circular-from-garray" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuentecantos and Chavaler - circular from Garray&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What natural attractions can I see along the no-traffic touring cycling routes?
A: The Duero region is rich in natural beauty. Along your rides, you might encounter dramatic river canyons, terraced vineyards, and diverse flora and fauna. Notable natural highlights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/745428" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Infante Dom Henrique Garden&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/409247" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gardens of the Crystal Palace&lt;/a&gt;. The region also features areas like the Arribes del Duero Natural Park, known for its deep gorges and unique microclimates.

Q: Are there any scenic viewpoints on these routes?
A: Absolutely! The Duero region is dotted with breathtaking viewpoints (miradouros) offering panoramic vistas of the river, canyons, and vineyards. You can find highlights like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3342444" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View from Cerro de Santa Ana&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5587359" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Andaluz Geodetic Vertex (1,027 m)&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4791099" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Staircase Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;, providing stunning photo opportunities and rest stops.

Q: What is the best time of year to cycle the no-traffic routes in the Duero region?
A: The best time to cycle in the Duero region is generally during spring (April to June) and autumn (September to October). During these seasons, the weather is mild and pleasant, ideal for cycling, and the landscapes are particularly beautiful with blooming flowers or vibrant autumn colors. Summers can be very hot, especially in the interior, while winters can be chilly and sometimes wet.

Q: Are there any routes suitable for families with children?
A: Yes, many of the easy and moderate no-traffic routes are suitable for families. These often follow riverside paths or quiet rural roads with minimal elevation, providing a safe and enjoyable experience for all ages. Look for routes with shorter distances and flatter profiles for the most family-friendly options.

Q: Can I bring my dog on these no-traffic touring cycling routes?
A: While many outdoor areas in the Duero region are dog-friendly, it's essential to check specific regulations for each route or natural park. Generally, dogs are welcome on leashes on public paths. However, always be mindful of local wildlife and other cyclists. Ensure your dog is comfortable with cycling speeds and distances.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about the Duero region's no-traffic routes?
A: The Duero region's no-traffic touring cycling routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars. Reviewers often praise the tranquility of the routes, the stunning river views, the charming historic towns along the way, and the opportunity to experience the region's famous vineyards and natural parks without the disturbance of traffic.

Q: Are there opportunities to see wildlife on these quiet cycling routes?
A: Yes, the Duero region, particularly in its natural parks like Arribes del Duero and Parque Natural do Douro Internacional, is home to diverse wildlife. On quieter routes, especially those along the riverbanks or through less populated areas, you might spot golden eagles, black storks, various waterfowl, and other local fauna. Cycling quietly increases your chances of wildlife encounters.

Q: Where can I find amenities like cafes or accommodation along these routes?
A: Many no-traffic routes pass through or near charming towns and villages where you can find cafes, restaurants, and accommodation. The Duero Valley is also famous for its quintas (wine estates), many of which offer tours, tastings, meals, and even lodging. Planning your route in advance and checking for amenities in towns like Fermoselle, Miranda do Douro, or Lamego is recommended.
tips: The Crestuma-Lever dam is located on the Douro River and came into operation in 1986. It is a mobile dam, made up of nine 25.5 meter high pillars.
Serra da Flores is part of the six mountains that make up the Parque das Serras do Porto, with almost 6,000 hectares in total. Its highest peak has an altitude of 318 meters.
